title: Is all sugar bad for you? High fructose corn syrup vs fruit & honey

chunks.title: Introduction to Sugars
chunks.summary: The podcast begins by discussing various types of sugars, particularly focusing on high fructose corn syrup (HFCS) compared to table sugar, honey, maple syrup, fruit, and starches. The host emphasizes the importance of understanding these differences and the implications for health.
chunks.transcript: different sources of sugar are different and in this podcast I want to focus mostly on high fructose corn syrup and how this one is different than table sugar then honey then maple syrup then fruit and then starches and why you should definitely get rid of high fructose corns here on this week's podcast I wanted to talk about sugar I wanted to talk about the differences in sugars and I wanted to talk about maybe why sugar foods like honey maple syrup dairy or fruit are not the same as high fructose corn syrup which is not the same as table sugar this is an interesting conversation and I begin the

chunks.title: Carbohydrates in Diets
chunks.summary: The speaker addresses concerns from keto and carnivore diet followers about including carbohydrates in their diets. They discuss the potential downsides of strictly following low-carb diets, such as increased cortisol levels and insulin resistance, while advocating for the inclusion of certain carbohydrate sources.
chunks.transcript: conversation with why you might even want to consider eating carbohydrates in your diet I know a lot of people that listen to my stuff have followed me from my strictly carnivore keto days and I hope this podcast will help fill in the gaps in that story and explain ideas and benefits of having carbohydrates in your diet I know many of you who are keto or carnivore fear including carbohydrates but I encourage you to listen to this podcast about downsides of a ketogenic diet could it age you faster there's evidence that it could does it increase your cortisol it does none of these are good things and the benefits of carbohydrates where I think you should get your carbohydrates and specific sources of carbohydrates that I think have been conflated in the literature with things like fruit honey maple syrup specifically high fructose corn syrup how it's harmful for humans and you definitely want to avoid that and help your kids avoid that but other sources of carbohydrates might not be so bad for you might actually improve the quality of your life so enjoy this podcast on fruit honey raw dairy maple syrup sugar and high fructose corn syrup and a little bit of discussion of ketogenic diets carbohydrates and why I think they're beneficial and why you might not want to do keto much longer are all

chunks.title: Understanding Insulin Resistance
chunks.summary: The discussion delves into the mechanics of how ketogenic diets can lead to physiological insulin resistance. The host argues that while insulin resistance occurs on low-carb diets, it differs from that seen in metabolic dysfunction or diabetes.

chunks.title: Debunking Myths About Carbs
chunks.summary: The podcast discusses misconceptions around carbohydrates, particularly the idea that they cause diabetes. The speaker references studies showing low-carb diets contributing to metabolic dysfunction rather than carbohydrates themselves.
chunks.transcript: glucose intolerance meaning that when you eat sugars or you eat carbohydrates your blood sugar may go up but that doesn't mean that those carbohydrates caused your diabetes that is a symptom rather than the cause and as I said in the last podcast on artificial sweeteners I think there has been too much Focus to the detriment of many people on glucose levels in the blood yes if your blood glucose goes to 180 milligrams per deciliter after eating that's probably too high 200 220. that's very high that's too many carbohydrates that's a problem but for those of you who are on a ketogenic diet or a carnivore diet and

chunks.summary: The host breaks down different types of sugars, explaining the nutritional profiles of honey, maple syrup, and fruit versus refined sugars like high fructose corn syrup. This segment emphasizes the importance of nutrient-rich sources versus empty-calorie sweeteners.

chunks.title: Natural Sweeteners vs. Processed Sugar
chunks.summary: The comparison between natural sweeteners like honey and processed sugars like HFCS continues. The host argues for the benefits of natural sugar sources and highlights the potential dangers of processed sugars that are stripped of nutrients.

chunks.title: Conclusion and Recommendations
chunks.summary: In the closing remarks, the speaker encourages listeners to focus on incorporating natural, nutrient-dense carbohydrates into their diets while avoiding high fructose corn syrup. They wrap up with a reminder of the longevity benefits of a balanced approach to carbohydrates.
